title: About Me
text:
I am a final-year Business Studies student at Dublin City University (DCU), specialising in Data Analytics, graduating in Summer 2026. I am tracking for First Class Honours based on my second and current year results.
I was born and raised in Kildare, and attended two Gaelscoils growing up in Kilcock and Lucan, achieving 566 points in my Leaving Certificate.
My interest in data analytics primarily comes from my 3rd year Intra placement in the digital team at PwC. During my 14 months there I had the opportunity to work on several very interesting analytics projects and gained experience with a wide range of tools. Going forward in my career I hope to continue using and devloping these skills working on meaningful projects.

title: New Enterprise Development — Mantis
text:
Mantis is a 500ml ready-to-drink chocolate protein shake made with Irish UHT milk, cricket powder, and buffalo worm powder. This project involved developing a full business plan for Mantis as part of the BAA1003 New Enterprise Development module at DCU, under the supervision of Dr. Eric Clinton.
The plan was developed by a five-person founding team, with me serving as CEO and product development lead.






Note



Mantis delivers 60 grams of protein per bottle (12g per 100ml) — the highest protein density of any RTD shake currently available on the Irish market. Competitors including Avonmore, GetPro, and MyProtein range from 6 to 9g per 100ml.

title: New Enterprise Development — Mantis
text:
Mantis is a 500ml ready-to-drink chocolate protein shake made with Irish UHT milk, cricket powder, and buffalo worm powder. This project involved developing a full business plan for Mantis as part of the BAA1003 New Enterprise Development module at DCU, under the supervision of Dr. Eric Clinton.
The plan was developed by a five-person founding team, with me serving as CEO and product development lead.






Note



Mantis delivers 60 grams of protein per bottle (12g per 100ml) — the highest protein density of any RTD shake currently available on the Irish market. Competitors including Avonmore, GetPro, and MyProtein range from 6 to 9g per 100ml.

title: New Enterprise Development — Mantis
text:
The Product
Both insect species used in Mantis are fully authorised under EU Novel Food regulations — crickets from February 2022 and buffalo worms from January 2023. The product is UHT shelf-stable, requiring no refrigeration until opened, and retails at €4.00 per bottle.
The recipe was developed through 27 iterations over the feasibility study period, with a blind taste test conducted with 26 participants. The final formulation achieved a 54% top-tier rating of 5/5.



Feature
Detail




Protein per bottle
60g (12g/100ml)


Retail price
€4.00


Wholesale price
€2.67


Shelf life
Ambient (UHT)


Protein source
Cricket + buffalo worm powder


CO₂ vs whey
6.5× lower per kg of protein

title: New Enterprise Development — Mantis
text:
Market Opportunity
The global protein supplement market is valued at approximately €20 billion, growing at 8–10% per annum. The Irish RTD protein beverage segment has seen 14.3% CAGR (2020–2024). Mantis identified a clear first-mover opportunity — no insect protein RTD drink currently exists in the Irish market.
Primary research (n=157 survey responses) confirmed that 77% of respondents would be open to trying an insect-protein drink, with taste and protein content identified as the primary purchase drivers.

title: New Enterprise Development — Mantis
text:
Financial Projections


Show projection code
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

years = ['Year 1 (2027)', 'Year 2 (2028)', 'Year 3 (2029)']
revenue = [93454, 542558, 2436056]
net_profit = [-23752, 73244, 562448]
units = [35045, 203459, 913521]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 5))

# Revenue and profit
x = np.arange(len(years))
axes[0].bar(x - 0.2, revenue, 0.4, label='Revenue (€)', color='#2e7dcc', alpha=0.85)
axes[0].bar(x + 0.2, net_profit, 0.4, label='Net Profit (€)', color='#0d1b2a', alpha=0.85)
axes[0].set_xticks(x)
axes[0].set_xticklabels(years)
axes[0].set_title('Revenue vs Net Profit (€)', fontsize=12)
axes[0].legend()
axes[0].yaxis.set_major_formatter(plt.FuncFormatter(lambda x, _: f'€{x:,.0f}'))

# Units sold
axes[1].plot(years, units, marker='o', color='#2e7dcc', linewidth=2.5)
axes[1].fill_between(years, units, alpha=0.1, color='#2e7dcc')
axes[1].set_title('Units Sold by Year', fontsize=12)
axes[1].yaxis.set_major_formatter(plt.FuncFormatter(lambda x, _: f'{x:,.0f}'))

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: New Enterprise Development — Mantis
text:
Go-to-Market Strategy
Distribution in Year 1 targets Holland & Barrett (14 Irish stores), Nourish Health Stores (20 stores), and independent gyms nationwide — placing the product on over 780 shelves. From Year 3, the plan includes expansion into national grocery multiples (Tesco Ireland and Dunnes Stores).

title: New Enterprise Development — Mantis
text:
Key Learnings
This project gave me practical experience across the full cycle of new venture development — from product ideation and consumer research through to financial modelling and investor pitch preparation. Taking the role of CEO taught me how to lead a multi-functional team, align competing priorities, and translate strategic goals into operational plans.

title: Project Management — Lyten Case Study
text:
This project is a case study analysis of Lyten, a US-based lithium-sulphur battery startup that acquired key assets from the bankrupt Swedish manufacturer Northvolt. The analysis was completed as part of the BAA1019 Business Strategy module at DCU.
The case study was structured around three questions examining strategic issues, internationalisation theory, and geopolitical alignment.

title: Project Management — Lyten Case Study
text:
This project is a case study analysis of Lyten, a US-based lithium-sulphur battery startup that acquired key assets from the bankrupt Swedish manufacturer Northvolt. The analysis was completed as part of the BAA1019 Business Strategy module at DCU.
The case study was structured around three questions examining strategic issues, internationalisation theory, and geopolitical alignment.

title: Project Management — Lyten Case Study
text:
Q1 — Strategic Issues Facing Lyten
Lyten’s acquisition presents several interconnected strategic challenges that will shape its long-term trajectory.






Note



Strategic issues in context: Critical decisions and challenges that influence a firm’s long-term direction, competitive advantage, and ability to create value (Johnson et al., 2017).


Post-acquisition integration is the most immediate challenge. Research suggests that many mergers fail due to cultural clashes, poor coordination, and difficulties in aligning processes (Zollo & Singh, 2004). The urgency of the Northvolt sale may have limited Lyten’s ability to conduct thorough due diligence.
Technology strategy presents a tension between Lyten’s core lithium-sulphur specialism and the lithium-ion production inherited from Northvolt. According to innovation strategy literature, firms must carefully balance exploration of new technologies with exploitation of existing capabilities (March, 1991).
Scope and market focus risks overextension across EVs, drones, and data centres simultaneously — a pattern that contributed to Northvolt’s own failure.
International expansion at pace, while simultaneously integrating complex assets, runs counter to the Uppsala model of gradual internationalisation (Johanson & Vahlne, 2009).
Institutional dependency on government support in Sweden, Germany, and potentially Canada creates strategic constraints alongside its benefits.

title: Project Management — Lyten Case Study
text:
Q2 — Internationalisation Strategy Assessment


Show strategic framework visualisation
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

# Uppsala model: gradual vs rapid expansion comparison
stages = ['Operational\nStabilisation', 'Regional\nConsolidation',
          'Sequential\nExpansion', 'Global\nScale']
recommended = [1, 2, 3, 4]
lyten_current = [0.3, 3.8, 4.0, 3.5]

x = np.arange(len(stages))
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5))
ax.plot(x, recommended, marker='o', label='Uppsala recommended path',
        color='#2e7dcc', linewidth=2.5)
ax.plot(x, lyten_current, marker='s', label="Lyten's current approach",
        color='#d94701', linewidth=2.5, linestyle='--')
ax.set_xticks(x)
ax.set_xticklabels(stages)
ax.set_ylabel('Intensity of activity')
ax.set_title("Lyten's Internationalisation vs Uppsala Model", fontsize=12)
ax.legend()
ax.set_ylim(0, 5)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()


Lyten’s expansion demonstrates features of strategic asset-seeking — acquiring Northvolt’s European facilities to access industrial infrastructure and skilled labour (Dunning, 1988). However, the pace of expansion is inconsistent with incremental internationalisation theory.
Research on premature scaling highlights that firms expanding too quickly without stable operational foundations face significant performance challenges (Reuber et al., 2021). Lyten is simultaneously integrating distressed assets, developing new battery technologies, and expanding into multiple geographic markets.
Recommended approach: A regional hub model, prioritising European stabilisation before North American commitment, with staged geographic expansion aligned to operational readiness.

title: Project Management — Lyten Case Study
section: Q3 — Geopolitical Alignment: Barrier or Constraint?
text:
Q3 — Geopolitical Alignment: Barrier or Constraint?
Lyten’s emphasis on local supply chains and government alignment can be evaluated through two lenses:







Perspective
Assessment




Barrier to entry
State-backed incentives and regulatory approvals favour embedded firms (Porter, 2008)


Institutional resource
Government alignment provides legitimacy and access to funding (Peng et al., 2009)


Strategic constraint
Over-dependence reduces flexibility and exposes firm to policy risk


Long-term vulnerability
Subsidies can be withdrawn; political priorities shift



The key trade-off is between leveraging institutional support for short-term competitive advantage and maintaining the strategic independence required to respond to market changes. Lyten should diversify its sources of support and ensure commercial logic — not political alignment — drives investment decisions.

title: Project Management — Lyten Case Study
text:
Frameworks Applied

Uppsala Internationalisation Model (Johanson & Vahlne, 2009)
Resource-Based View (Barney, 1991)
Porter’s Five Forces (Porter, 2008)
Institutional Theory (Peng et al., 2009)
Exploration/Exploitation Framework (March, 1991)

title: Project Management — Lyten Case Study
text:
Key Learnings
This project deepened my ability to apply theoretical frameworks to real strategic situations. Analysing Lyten’s position required balancing multiple competing perspectives — which mirrors the complexity of real strategic decision-making — and reinforced the value of structured frameworks in cutting through ambiguous, multi-faceted problems.

title: Machine Learning & Advanced Python
text:
This project involved building and evaluating three machine learning models to predict the likelihood of stroke in patients, using a real-world medical dataset. The task required handling a heavily imbalanced dataset — approximately 20:1 in favour of non-stroke cases — and producing models that could maximise recall of true stroke cases without sacrificing overall performance.






Note



Dataset: Stroke Prediction Dataset — 5,110 patient records with features including age, BMI, average glucose level, smoking status, hypertension, and work type.

title: Machine Learning & Advanced Python
text:
This project involved building and evaluating three machine learning models to predict the likelihood of stroke in patients, using a real-world medical dataset. The task required handling a heavily imbalanced dataset — approximately 20:1 in favour of non-stroke cases — and producing models that could maximise recall of true stroke cases without sacrificing overall performance.






Note



Dataset: Stroke Prediction Dataset — 5,110 patient records with features including age, BMI, average glucose level, smoking status, hypertension, and work type.

title: Machine Learning & Advanced Python
text:
Tools & Libraries



Library
Purpose




pandas / numpy
Data manipulation and preprocessing


scikit-learn
Model training, evaluation, GridSearchCV


imbalanced-learn
SMOTE oversampling


xgboost
XGBoost classifier


seaborn / matplotlib
Data visualisation

title: Machine Learning & Advanced Python
text:
Methodology

1. Exploratory Data Analysis
The dataset was first examined for structure, missing values, and class imbalance. BMI had 201 missing values, which were imputed using the median. A single record with gender “Other” was removed.


Show EDA code
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Load and inspect the dataset
file = '/content/strokedataset.csv'
data = pd.read_csv(file)

print("Dataset shape:", data.shape)
print("\nMissing values:\n", data.isna().sum())
print("\nClass distribution:\n", data['stroke'].value_counts())
print(f"\nClass imbalance ratio: {data['stroke'].value_counts()[0] / data['stroke'].value_counts()[1]:.1f}:1")


The dataset was significantly imbalanced, with only 4.87% of cases being positive stroke events — a common challenge in medical datasets that requires careful handling to avoid models that simply predict “no stroke” for every patient.


2. Preprocessing


Show preprocessing code
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.preprocessing import LabelEncoder, StandardScaler
from imblearn.over_sampling import SMOTE

# Impute missing BMI with median
data['bmi'] = pd.to_numeric(data['bmi'], errors='coerce')
data['bmi'] = data['bmi'].fillna(data['bmi'].median())

# Drop ID and remove 'Other' gender
data.drop(columns=['id'], inplace=True)
data = data[data['gender'] != 'Other']

# Encode categorical variables
le = LabelEncoder()
for col in ['gender', 'ever_married', 'work_type', 'Residence_type', 'smoking_status']:
    data[col] = le.fit_transform(data[col])

# Split, scale, and apply SMOTE
X = data.drop('stroke', axis=1)
y = data['stroke']

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.2, stratify=y, random_state=42)

scaler = StandardScaler()
X_train_sc = scaler.fit_transform(X_train)
X_test_sc = scaler.transform(X_test)

smote = SMOTE(sampling_strategy=0.3, k_neighbors=5, random_state=42)
X_train_sm, y_train_sm = smote.fit_resample(X_train_sc, y_train)


SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique) was applied with a sampling strategy of 0.3, generating synthetic stroke cases to reduce the class imbalance in the training set without duplicating existing records.


3. Model Training
Three classifiers were trained and compared:


Show model training code
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV
from xgboost import XGBClassifier

# Logistic Regression
log_reg = LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000, class_weight='balanced', random_state=42)
log_reg.fit(X_train_sm, y_train_sm)

# Random Forest with GridSearchCV
param_grid_rf = {
    'n_estimators': [100, 200],
    'max_depth': [4, 6, None],
    'min_samples_leaf': [2, 5],
    'class_weight': ['balanced']
}
grid_rf = GridSearchCV(
    RandomForestClassifier(random_state=42),
    param_grid_rf, scoring='roc_auc', cv=5, n_jobs=-1)
grid_rf.fit(X_train_sm, y_train_sm)
best_rf = grid_rf.best_estimator_

# XGBoost
xgb = XGBClassifier(
    scale_pos_weight=10, max_depth=4, learning_rate=0.05,
    n_estimators=200, min_child_weight=3, gamma=0.2,
    subsample=0.8, colsample_bytree=0.8,
    eval_metric='logloss', random_state=42, verbosity=0)
xgb.fit(X_train_sm, y_train_sm)

title: Machine Learning & Advanced Python
text:
Results



Model
Accuracy
Precision
Recall
F1-Score
ROC-AUC




Logistic Regression
0.7423
0.1891
0.7143
0.2993
0.7912


Random Forest
0.9141
0.3448
0.2857
0.3125
0.8203


XGBoost
0.8200
0.2174
0.5714
0.3143
0.7834









Tip



Key finding: Random Forest achieved the highest ROC-AUC (0.82), making it the strongest overall performer. For a medical screening context where missing a stroke is more costly than a false alarm, Logistic Regression’s higher recall (0.71) may be preferable despite lower precision.

title: Machine Learning & Advanced Python
text:
Key Learnings
Working with this project reinforced the importance of understanding the business context behind model selection — in healthcare, recall often matters more than accuracy. It also highlighted the practical challenges of class imbalance and the value of techniques like SMOTE and class_weight='balanced' in producing fair models on skewed data.

title: Reflection
text:
This ePortfolio represents the culmination of my final year of study in Business Studies at Dublin City University. Building it has given me an opportunity to look back on what I have learned to prepare me for a career in analytics.






Note



This reflection covers my experience across some of the key skills I have developed during this degree including analytics entrepreneurship, and strategy.

title: Reflection
text:
This ePortfolio represents the culmination of my final year of study in Business Studies at Dublin City University. Building it has given me an opportunity to look back on what I have learned to prepare me for a career in analytics.






Note



This reflection covers my experience across some of the key skills I have developed during this degree including analytics entrepreneurship, and strategy.

title: Reflection
text:
Data Analytics & Machine Learning
Before starting this degree I had no programming experience. Learning Python from scratch was one of the steepest learning curves I faced, but it has become one of the most valuable skills I have developed.
The machine learning project, building stroke prediction models using logistic regression, random forest, and XGBoost, pushed me well beyond the basics. Working with a real medical dataset forced me to confront practical challenges such as handling missing data, dealing with severe class imbalance, and making model selection decisions that reflect the actual cost of errors in a healthcare context.
One of the most important things I learned is that clean, well-documented code matters as much as the results it produces. Writing code that another analyst could read and understand, by using clear variable names, comments, and structured sections, is a professional habit I will carry into my career.

title: Reflection
text:
Web Development — Quarto & Git
Building this site was my first real experience with version control and static site publishing. This project required a deep understanding of how repositories, branches, commits, and deployment pipelines work together.
The process was not without difficulty. I encountered Jekyll conflicts, deployment errors, broken paths, and duplicate build artifacts, each of which required diagnosing the root cause rather than just trying random fixes. Working through those issues gave me a much stronger mental model of how web publishing actually works.





flowchart LR
    A[Write .qmd files] --> B[quarto render]
    B --> C[docs/ folder generated]
    C --> D[git add & commit]
    D --> E[git push]
    E --> F[GitHub Pages deploys]
    F --> G[Live website]

title: Reflection
text:
New Enterprise Development — Mantis
The Mantis project was one of the most demanding and rewarding experiences of my degree. Developing a full business plan from concept to investor pitch, across product development, market research, financial modelling, and regulatory compliance, required integrating skills from across the entire degree.
Taking the role of CEO and product development lead meant I was responsible for the overall direction of the plan while also coordinating contributions from four teammates. This tested my ability to manage competing priorities, give and receive constructive feedback, and maintain momentum across a long project.

title: Reflection
text:
Business Strategy
The Lyten case study was an exercise in applying theoretical frameworks to a real, ambiguous strategic situation. Analysing a company that was simultaneously integrating distressed assets, managing competing technologies, and expanding internationally required holding multiple perspectives at once — something that does not come naturally and takes practice.
Working with frameworks like the Uppsala model, Resource-Based View, and Porter’s Five Forces taught me that theory is most useful when it is treated as a lens rather than a checklist. The best analysis I produced came when I used frameworks to structure my thinking, not to replace it.

title: Reflection
text:
Professional Experience — PwC Digital Team
My co-op at PwC from June 2024 to July 2025 was the single most formative experience of my degree. Working within the Digital Team gave me direct exposure to how large organisations adopt new technologies, and how difficult that process is in practice.
Facilitating ideation workshops, building Power Automate solutions, creating Power BI dashboards, and supporting the M365 migration all required me to move quickly between technical and interpersonal modes, explaining tool functionality to senior stakeholders one moment, and debugging an asset the next.
Having my contract extended to onboard and train new interns was something I was particularly proud of. It confirmed that the skills and knowledge I had built over the placement were genuinely valued, and gave me early experience of teaching and knowledge transfer.

title: Reflection
text:
Looking Forward
I am graduating in Summer 2026 with a clearer sense of the career I want going forward. I am most energised by work that sits at the intersection of technical analysis and strategic decision-making, using data to answer questions that actually matter to an organisation, and communicating those answers in a way that drives action.
The skills I have built during this degree, Python, SQL, Power BI, Power Automate, machine learning, business strategy, and financial modelling, give me a strong foundation. But more than any individual skill, I leave DCU with a way of approaching problems: breaking them down, finding the right framework, building something rigorous, and communicating it clearly.
